segments.s401-g105: سپاهش is سپاه + possessive suffix -ش (“his army”); Persian often attaches possessive -ش to the noun. همه مانده is a copular/result phrase: مانده (past participle / past-tense participial form) here means “was left / stood” (they remained in that state). زو is a poetic contraction of ز + او (from/at him), so ز + او + در شگفت means “at him, in wonder” — در شگفت is the idiom “in astonishment.” Word order is Subject (سپاهش) + adverbial/secondary predicate (همه مانده زو در شگفت) describing their state.
segments.s401-g106: که introduces the subordinate clause (“that/for” — here giving the reason they were astonished). مرد جوان is a noun + adjective phrase (man + young). آن is a demonstrative emphasising the particular quality (“that / such”). دلیری گرفت is a light-verb construction: دلیری (bravery) + گرفت (took/seized) — idiomatically “displayed / showed bravery”; گرفت is past-tense 3rd-person singular.
segments.s401-g41: همه بارگاهش is the subject phrase ('his whole palace/court') with the possessive suffix -ش on بارگاه. چنان شد is a predicative phrase meaning 'became such' (چنان = 'so/such', شد = past/resultative 'became'). که introduces the following subordinate clause that describes the manner/result ('that...'). راه here is the noun 'path/way' and is syntactically the item whose absence will be stated in the next segment.
segments.s401-g42: نبود is the past negative/existential 'there was not/was no' (here used in narrative past). اندران is an older locative ('in/among that'), locating the negation, and نامور بارگاه is the noun phrase 'that famed court/palace'. Together this yields 'there was no [way] in that renowned court.'
segments.s401-g43: ز بس is a poetic causal phrase meaning 'because of so many / from such abundance'; it introduces a list of states describing people/bodies (elliptically understood). خسته و کشته و کوفته are three coordinated predicates/adjectives ('tired/exhausted', 'killed/slain', 'crushed/battered') enumerating the condition of those present. The conjunctions و simply join the three terms.
segments.s401-g44: زمین is the subject 'the earth/ground'. همچو introduces a simile 'like/as', followed by دریای آشوفته 'a disturbed/upset sea' as the comparator; this is a verbless simile predicated of the ground, describing its churned appearance after the slaughter.
segments.s401-g45: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. ارجاسپ is the proper-name subject. از خواب بیدار شد is a verbal phrase 'awoke from sleep' where از + خواب marks source ('from sleep') and بیدار شد is a past/resultative periphrasis ('became awake' = 'woke up'); شد functions as the auxiliary/result marker in narrative past.
segments.s401-g46: ز غلغل is an ablative/casual phrase 'from/because of the clamor/uproar'. دلش is the possessed noun 'his heart' (دل + possessive -ش). پر ز تیمار شد is the predicate 'became full of care/anxiety' (پر = 'full (of)', ز تیمار = 'of care', شد = 'became'), so the whole segment means his heart was filled with solicitude because of the noise.
segments.s401-g47: بجوشید is a figurative intransitive verb 'boiled/seethed' (here 'seethed with rage' or 'sprang up'); in the poem the verb precedes the subject (poetic inversion). ارجاسپ is the subject, and از جایگاه is an adverbial phrase 'from his place/seat', indicating from where he rose.
segments.s401-g48: بپوشید is the past action 'he put on/donned'. It takes the coordinated objects خفتان و رومی کلاه: two items joined by و. رومی modifies کلاه ('Roman hat/helmet'). Note: the provided lexical senses list does not give a matching sense for خفتان in this context (a garment/boot), so its sense index is marked -1.
segments.s401-g65: A noun phrase serving as the sentence's topic/predicate: 'چنین است' = 'such is' (copula + demonstrative), followed by 'کردار' (head noun 'deed/behaviour') modified by 'گردنده دهر' (a participial adjective + noun: 'the turning/revolving world'). Word order is topical (demonstrative+copula then the descriptive noun phrase).
segments.s401-g66: 'گهی ... گاه ...' is a paired adverbial construction meaning 'sometimes ... at other times ...'. 'نوش یابیم' is a stative/result clause with یابیم (first‑person plural present/subjunctive used as a general present: 'we obtain/find'); نوش here means 'pleasant thing/sweetness, delight'. 'ازو' = 'from it/of it'. 'گاه زهر' = 'at times (we get) poison' (زهر = 'poison').
segments.s401-g67: 'چه بندی' is an interrogative/exclamatory phrase: چه ('how/what') + بندی (2nd‑person singular present of بستن: 'you bind/close'), so 'what/why do you bind...'. 'دل' = 'heart'. 'اندر' = preposition 'in/within'. 'سرای سپنج' is a compound where سرای means 'house/place' (here 'house') and سپنج 'sponge' — 'a sponge-house' (poetic image); the noun phrase is the object/location of بندی.
segments.s401-g68: 'چو دانی' = 'since/as you know' (چو = 'when/as'; دانی = 2nd‑person singular present of دانستن: 'you know'). 'که' introduces the content clause 'that ...'. 'ایدر نمانی' = 'you will not remain here' — نمانی is the negative form of ماندن (2nd person: 'you do not/will not remain'). 'مرنج' is the negative imperative 2nd person singular of رنجیدن: 'do not be upset'. The whole clause gives a reason and the following admonition.
segments.s401-g69: بپرداخت is a past‑tense verb (3rd‑person singular) meaning 'he dealt with/paid (back, i.e. avenged)'. In this poetic word order the agent/name 'اسفندیار' follows the verb phrase; 'ز ارجاسپ' marks the object/target (ز used poetically as a preposition 'upon/against' or 'from' in the sense 'with respect to Arjasp').
segments.s401-g70: 'به کیوان برآورد' is a verbal phrase: به + کیوان ('toward/to Kayvān/the heavens') + برآورد (past 3rd‑singular of برآوردن, here 'he brought/raised/caused to go up'). 'ز ایوان دمار' is an ablative/partitive phrase ('from the iwan, [he] (brought) d̲amār' — دمار = 'ruin/destruction'). Together: he raised/brought destruction from the iwan toward Kayvān; classical Persian allows such compact, elliptical phrasing.
segments.s401-g71: بفرمود is a polite/honorific past verb meaning 'he commanded/said'. تا introduces the subordinate clause of purpose/result ('so that/that'), here 'تا شمع بفروختند' — شمع = 'candles' (object) and بفروختند is past plural 'they sold' (i.e. he ordered that candles be sold/distributed). The construction is an ordered action reported in the past.
segments.s401-g72: 'به هر سوی ایوان' is a locative phrase: به = 'to/toward', هر سوی = 'every direction/side', ایوان = 'the iwan/porch'. 'همی سوختند' is past plural 'they burned' with the classical particle همی giving an emphatic/continuous tone; together: 'they burned on every side of the iwan' (referring to the candles lit/sold).
segments.s401-g97: This phrase is a sequence of adpositional and nominal phrases setting the circumstance: به + پیروزی = 'on/for the victory' (به marking an adverbial goal or occasion). از + باره = 'on the occasion of / concerning the instance' (از باره is a poetic locution meaning 'on the occasion of'), followed by کاخ (palace). پاس here is a postpositional/relational word meaning 'in return for / on account of' that links the whole phrase as the motivating occasion for what follows.
segments.s401-g98: بدارید is an imperative/hortative form meaning 'hold/offer' (2pl polite/poetic imperative) that governs the following object. از marks the target of the gratitude (here functioning like English 'to' or 'for' in 'thank X'), پاک یزدان is a determiner + divine noun 'the Pure God', and سپاس is the direct object 'thanks/praise' placed after the prepositional phrase for poetic word order: 'offer thanks to the Pure God.'
segments.s401-g99: سر is the noun 'head' (literal, here the decapitated head) followed by the genitive compound شاه ترکان = 'king of the Turks' (شاه governing ترکان). ازان دیدگاه is a postpositional phrase 'from that sight / from that vantage' where ازان = 'from that' modifies دیدگاه ('viewpoint, sight'); the whole phrase names the object (the head) and situates the action in relation to a viewpoint.
segments.s401-g100: بینداخت is a past-form verb 'threw/cast'; when combined with باید that follows here, it expresses necessity in narrative (poetic inversion: verb before modal), roughly 'it is necessary that he cast/threw'. باید = 'must/ought' gives obligation, به پیش سپاه = 'before/in the presence of the army' (به + پیش = 'to/in front of', سپاه = 'army') shows the place/recipient of the action.
segments.s401-g101: بیامد is the past tense 'came'. ز دژ = 'from the fortress' (ز as the poetic form of از, indicating origin). با + صد و شست مرد = 'with one hundred and sixty men'; صد و شست is a numeric phrase (century + sixty) giving the number of men and is the complement of با. The numeral شست here is the classical word for sixty (note: that sense is not listed in the provided senses and so is marked -1).
segments.s401-g102: خروشان و جوشان are participial/adjectival manner-words 'roaring' and 'seething/boiling' placed before the destination to describe how they came; they function adverbially here. به دشت نبرد = 'to the plain/field of battle' (به marking direction, دشت 'plain', نبرد as 'battle' specifying the field). Together they describe the manner and destination of the arrival.
segments.s401-g103: چو = 'when' introducing a temporal clause. نزد سپاه پشوتن = 'near/in the presence of the army of Peshotan' where نزد is a preposition 'near/in the presence of', سپاه is 'army', and پشوتن is the proper name (postposed to form the genitive 'Peshotan's army'). رسید is the past tense verb 'arrived/reached'.
segments.s401-g104: برو here functions as a discourse particle/short exclamation often rendered 'thereupon' in epic narration (though its dictionary sense is 'go'). نامدار 'renowned' modifies the implied person; آفرین 'acclamation/"bravo"' is the praise, and گسترید (from گستراندن) 'spread/utter' is a perfect/past or narrative-present verb meaning 'he spread/cried out' — together: 'thereupon he uttered acclamation to the renowned one.'
segments.s401-g33: چنین is an adverb meaning 'thus/so' placed before the verb for emphasis. گفت is the past 3rd-person singular 'said' (here introducing direct speech). با خواهران شیرمرد is the prepositional phrase 'to/with the sisters of the brave man' showing the addressees of the speech.
segments.s401-g34: کز is a relative/connector 'that/who/from which' introducing what follows. ایدر = 'here'. بپویید is a 2nd-person-plural hortative/imperative form (ب- + پویید) meaning 'go/seek, search' addressed to the sisters. برسان is an imperative 'bring/deliver' and گرد here works as an archaic verbal particle meaning 'go about/make it so' (used with imperatives), so together they give the command 'seek from here and bring (it).'
segments.s401-g35: بدانجا = 'to/at that place'; که introduces the relative clause 'which/that'. بازارگاه is 'marketplace' (the noun being described) and منست is a predicative phrase (here written contracted) meaning 'is mine / is my (place)'; in this poetic register منست functions as the copular phrase.
segments.s401-g36: بسی زر و سیم است = 'there is much/plenty of gold and silver' where بسی modifies the quantity, زر و سیم are the nouns, and است is the copula. و گاه منست links with another predicate: گاه here means 'place/seat' (poetic use) and منست again functions as the copula/predicative 'is mine / is fitting'.
segments.s401-g37: مباشید is a negative imperative (2nd-person plural) meaning 'do not be / do not stay'. با من is 'with me' and بدین رزمگاه = 'to/in this battlefield' (بدین = به + این, 'to/at this'). The whole segment is a prohibition: 'Do not be with me in this battlefield.'
segments.s401-g38: اگر ... گر is an older Persian conditional pattern: اگر introduces the protasis ('if'), and the postposed گر marks the apodosis/result clause. دهم‌گر = 'if I give' (دهم + گر) with سر 'head' as its object: 'if I give my head'. ستانم is the 1st-person verb 'I take/snatch' (apodosis), and کلاه is its object 'helmet/hat'. The line poses the conditional consequence: 'if I give my head, will I take the helmet?'
segments.s401-g39: بیامد is the past tense 'came'. یکی here functions as 'a certain/one (man)'. تیغ هندی = 'Indian sword' (noun + adjective). به مشت = 'in (his) fist' (به + مشت, a locative/expressive phrase meaning 'brandishing/in hand'). The sequence describes someone arriving brandishing an Indian blade.
segments.s401-g40: کسی را is the definite/affected object 'someone' marked by را. که دید introduces a relative clause 'whom he saw'. از دلیران means 'from/of the brave ones' (i.e. one of the champions). بکشت is the past tense 'killed' (archaic/dialectal form). So the whole segment: 'he killed someone whom he saw to be among the brave.'
segments.s401-g1: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a time clause. تاریکتر شد is comparative adjective + شد ('became'): 'became darker'; شب is the noun 'night' and is the subject of this clause. اسفندیار is the subject of the following main verb (held after the time clause) and is placed here for poetic order.
segments.s401-g2: بپوشید is a past (narrative) form meaning 'he put on / he donned' (the prefix ب- appears in classical/poetic forms). نو modifies جامه ('a new garment'); جامه کارزار is a compound 'garment of battle' = 'battle-robe'. The verb appears before its object here owing to poetic word order.
segments.s401-g3: سر بند is a compound meaning 'top/fastening' or 'lid/lock' (سر = top; بند = fastening), صندوقها is the plural 'the chests/boxes', and برگشاد is a past participial/past narrative form 'opened' (bar-gashâd). Together: 'he opened the chests' lids/fastenings.'
segments.s401-g4: یکی = 'one' or 'someone' (introduces a person). تا ... here functions roughly as 'up to/toward'; ب is the prepositional element 'to/into' and joins with the following item to indicate direction; دان in the manuscript segmentation does not match the usual verbal sense and here functions as part of the directional phrase (textual/archaic form), بستگان = 'the relatives/kinsmen', جست = 'he leapt/sprang' (past), and باد is the optative/jussive particle 'may' often used in classical narrative formulae. The whole gives 'one sprang toward those relatives' with an archaic/jussive flavor.
segments.s401-g5: کباب is the noun 'kebab' (food). و is the coordinating 'and'. می‌آورد is a narrative-form verb 'he brought / would bring' (present-imperfect used in narrative to mean 'brought' or 'was bringing'). و connects to نوشیدنی 'beverage/drink' which is the second item in the list.
segments.s401-g6: همان = 'that very / the same' or 'those', qualifying جامه رزم = 'war-garment / battle-robe' (جامه = garment; رزم = battle/war). و joins پوشیدنی, here used as a noun/nominal meaning 'things to be worn' or 'clothing' (i.e., additional garments). The phrase lists the clothing items taken out.
segments.s401-g7: چو = 'when' introducing a temporal clause. نان خورده شد is a perfect/passive construction ('the bread was eaten' or 'when the bread had been eaten') — خورده is the past participle and شد is the auxiliary 'became/was'. هر یکی را marks the beneficiary/object 'to each one' (را as object marker) and سه جام is the quantity 'three cups' that will be given (verb appears in the next segment).
segments.s401-g8: بدادند is the past 3rd-person plural 'they gave'. و = 'and'. گشتند here means 'they became' (intransitive past of گشتن used as 'become'). زان = 'from that / because of that' (referring back to the gift/act), and شادکام is an adjective 'joyful/fortunate'. Together: 'they gave (them) and thereby became glad.'
segments.s401-g89: This is an imperative clause: ببندید is a 2nd-person plural imperative 'shut/close/lock up' placed at the end as in classical Persian word order. به ترکان در دژ together indicate the patient/location: به + ترکان points to the people to be confined ('the Turks') and در دژ gives the place 'in the fortress'. سخت is an adverb modifying ببندید meaning 'firmly/securely'.
segments.s401-g90: مگر here functions as 'unless'. یار باشد مرا is a verbless/existential construction with باشد (3rd-person subjunctive/optative 'may there be' / 'be') and مرا (here split orthographically between م and را) marking 'for me' / 'me' as beneficiary/object: literally 'a companion be for me'. نیک‌بخت is a vocative/epithet 'O fortunate one' addressing someone in the speech; it qualifies the addressee.
segments.s401-g91: هرانگه که آید is a temporal formula 'whenever it happens/when it comes to pass'. گمانتان is 'your thought/belief' (گمان + enclitic -تان). The second که introduces the subordinate clause 'that I ...' and من is the 1st-person subject 'I'. The clause order is topical: the time clause + your thought + that + subject.
segments.s401-g92: رسیدم is past tense 'I arrived/reached'. بدَان is composed of ب (preposition 'to/into') + دان (here functioning as 'that'/'to that' in the text), so together mean 'to that'. پاک‌رای is an adjectival epithet 'pure-reputation/virtuous' modifying انجمن 'assembly/meeting'. The phrase literally: 'I arrived to that virtuous assembly.'
segments.s401-g93: غو دیده باید is an impersonal/periphrastic construction: غو 'tumult' is the subject/topic, دیده باید 'must be seen' (دیده = 'seen' / noun from 'to see'; باید = 'it is necessary' / 'must'). که introduces the following prepositional phrase; از دیدگاه = 'from the viewpoint/vantage of'. Word order is topicalizing the noise/tumult as what must be observed.
segments.s401-g94: This is a noun-chain specifying the vantage-point: کانوشه is 'the little chest/casket' (container), سر و تاج 'head and crown' are its contents or items associated, followed by the name گشتاسپ and the title شاه. The sequence is genitive/attributive: 'the casket of the head and crown of King Gushtasp.'
segments.s401-g95: چو introduces a temporal/conditional clause 'when/if'. انبوه گردد is 'becomes numerous / the crowd forms' (گردد = becomes/happens). به دژ gives the location 'in/at the fortress'. بر سپاه means 'against the army' (بر here 'against/upon' indicating opposition). Word order places location before the target noun as is common in Persian epic usage.
segments.s401-g96: This is a verbless nominal series describing the state/result: گریز ان (گریزان) 'fleeing' and برگشته 'returned / turned back' followed by از رزمگاه 'from the battlefield'. The adjectives/participles summarize the consequence: they are fleeing and have turned back from the fight.
segments.s401-g9: چنین گفت is a fixed narrative formula: چنین (adverb 'thus') precedes the past verb گفت (third-person singular of گفتن) and together mean 'thus said' or 'he said thus'. The following phrase کامشب شبی پربلاست is a verbless predication: کامشب ('this/tonight') + شبی ('a night') + پربلاست (adjective 'full of calamity') — the copula 'is' is omitted, so the clause asserts 'tonight is a night full of affliction.'
segments.s401-g10: اگر introduces a conditional clause. نام گیریم uses گیریم (subjunctive/present first-person-plural form of گرفتن) with نام meaning 'reputation/name,' so 'اگر نام گیریم' = 'if we gain/earn a name (reputation)'. ز ایدر is a poetic prepositional phrase ('from/ere/here'), and سزاست is a present copular/predicative 'it is deserved/just/fitting.' The construction yields 'if we gain a name from here, it is deserved.'
segments.s401-g11: بکوشید is an imperative/hortative plural ('strive!' or 'make effort'), followed by the coordinating conjunction و ('and') which connects a second imperative-like clause. پیکار ... کنید is a light-verb construction: پیکار (noun 'battle') with کردن (کنید, present/imperfective/imperative 2/plural) yields 'engage in battle / fight.' مردان is the object (or adverbial quality 'like men') attached to پیکار, so the clause commands 'strive, and fight like men.'
segments.s401-g12: پناه ... کنید is another light-verb phrase where پناه (refuge) with کردن (کنید, imperative/optative) gives 'take refuge' or 'seek refuge.' از بلاها is the source phrase 'from the calamities' and به یزدان is the beneficiary/locative 'in/with God.' Word order places the source before the beneficiary: 'seek refuge from calamities in God.'
segments.s401-g13: ازان پس is an adverbial phrase meaning 'after that' or 'thereupon.' The main clause is یلان را به سه بهر کرد: یلان را (direct object marked by را, 'the heroes') + به سه بهر (prepositional phrase 'into three portions/shares') + کرد (past tense third-person singular of کردن, 'he made/divided'). The verb کرد narrates the action of dividing them into three shares.
segments.s401-g14: هرانکس که introduces a relative clause 'whoever that...' with که as the relativizer. جستند is past plural and here functions as 'sought' (verb + object), taking ننگ و نبرد as its objects: two coordinated nouns meaning (in this context) 'renown/honour' and 'battle' — an idiomatic pairing. The clause is thus 'whoever sought renown and battle.'
segments.s401-g15: یکی بهره is a numeric phrase: یکی ('one') qualifies بهره ('share' or portion). زیشان ('of them') is a genitive/possessive clitic attached to بهره. میان حصار is a locative expression 'in/among the ramparts/within the fortress.' Together they state 'one share of them (was) inside the fortress.'
segments.s401-g16: که here introduces a relative/purpose clause modifying 'one share': سازند is third-person plural (present/subjunctive/optative form) of ساختن/ساختن used narratively to indicate 'who would/should make' or more simply 'who make/engage in.' با هرکسی is 'with anyone/whomever they meet,' and کارزار (with کردن implied) means 'battle/war.' The clause therefore reads 'so that they make war with anyone they encounter.'
segments.s401-g25: به درگاه ارجاسپ آمد دلیر is a fronted prepositional phrase + verb + predicative noun: 'به درگاه ارجاسپ' (to/into Arjasp’s court) uses به + place, آمد is the past tense verb 'came', and دلیر follows the verb as the nominal description of the one who came (poetic inversion: subject/epithet after the verb).
segments.s401-g26: زره‌دار و غران به کردار شیر is a coordinate description attached to that subject: زره‌دار (an armored warrior) and غران (roaring) are paired qualities, while به کردار شیر is a manner phrase 'in the conduct/manner of a lion' with به used to mark manner ('by/‑ly') and کردار meaning 'behavior, conduct'.
segments.s401-g27: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. زخم خروش is a poetic compound describing a sharp/striking cry (here treated as a single dramatic sound), آمد is the past verb 'came', and از در سرای is a place source 'from the door of the hall' where از marks origin and در here means 'door'. (The lemma list for سرای in the provided data does not give the expected sense 'hall', so that word is irregular in the supplied senses.)
segments.s401-g28: دوان is a verbal/adverbial form 'running/rushing', پیش آزادگان is the prepositional phrase 'before/in the presence of the nobles' (پیش = before/in front of), شد is the past auxiliary/verb indicating the occurrence (here functioning like 'came to be / rushed'), and همای (the name) appears at the end as the subject (poetic inversion: subject after the verb).
segments.s401-g29: ابا here functions as a proper name (not the lexical sense 'to be reluctant'); خواهر خویش 'his sister' is a possessed noun phrase (خویش = 'his/one’s own', split into خوی + ش), and به آفرید uses به + آفرید 'created/brought forth' (آفرید = past 'created/made'). Word order places the name first, then the direct object phrase, then the verb.
segments.s401-g30: به خون مژه is an instrumental/causal phrase 'with/by the blood of the eyelashes' (به marking the means), کرده is a perfect/participial form 'having made / made', رخ ناپدید means 'made (his) face disappear' (رخ = face/appearance, ناپدید = vanished/invisible). Together this gives a causative result: by that bleeding he hid/obliterated his face.
segments.s401-g31: چو آمد is the temporal 'when he came/drew near'. به تنگ اندر is an adverbial/locative phrase: به تنگ (to/into the narrow place) with اندر (archaic 'in/within'), and اسفندیار is the name in genitive/locative relation — overall 'when he came into the narrow space before Esfandiyar' or simply 'when he drew near Esfandiyar'.
segments.s401-g32: دو پوشیده را دید is straightforward: 'he saw two covered/veiled ones' with را marking the definite direct object and دید the past verb 'saw'. چون نوبهار is a simile placed after the verb: 'like the new spring' (comparing their appearance to springtime).
segments.s401-g17: دگر بهره is a noun phrase meaning 'the other contingent/party' (دگر before بهره = 'other'). تا is the subordinating conjunction 'until' governing the following clause; بر در دژ شوند is a predicative clause with شوند (third‑person plural subjunctive/present of شدن) meaning 'become/come to be' or more naturally 'be/arrive at'. Word order is typical Persian: temporal/concessive تا + clause, with the locative phrase بر در دژ ('at/on the gate of the fortress') placed before the verb.
segments.s401-g18: ز here introduces the cause/source and functions like 'from' or 'in respect of'. The coordination پیکار و خون ریختن is a noun phrase 'battle and the shedding of blood' (خون ریختن = 'blood‑shedding'). نغنوند is a poetic/archaic verbal form from غنودن meaning 'to slumber/abate' — with the negative prefix ن‑ here giving the sense 'did not slumber/cease' (i.e. 'did not stop fighting').
segments.s401-g19: This clause has گفت 'said/told' as the main verb with بهره را as a (definite) object phrase 'the contingent' (را marks definiteness/object). از introduces the following noun as the topic of the report ('about' / 'concerning'). سرکشان is a plural noun here (likely 'the rebellious/insubordinate ones' or 'the chiefs' depending on context) and functions as the object of از; the given lemma senses for سرکشان in the dataset don't match this use.
segments.s401-g20: This is a reported content clause introduced by که 'that'. باید is a modal 'must/it is necessary', followed by a second که introducing an infinitival/subjunctive clause with یابید (subjunctive/imperative form of یافتن) meaning 'you should/find'. زیشان is the archaic compound prepositional phrase 'from them' and نشان is the noun 'sign/trace'. Overall: 'that (you) must find a sign from them.'
segments.s401-g21: This relative/explanatory clause starts with که 'that/who'. بودند is the past plural 'they were' (indicating a prior state). با ما 'with us' is a prepositional phrase. ز + می‌دوش مست is a terse poetic expression: ز introduces the source 'from', می‌دوش is here a noun/verb form related to 'milk/milking' (literally 'milk' or 'to milk') and مست 'drunk/intoxicated' follows, so together they mean 'drunk from milk' or 'intoxicated by milking' — a compressed epic phrasing describing their state.
segments.s401-g22: سرانشان is the object 'their chiefs/heads'. به introduces the instrument or means ('by/with') and خنجر is 'dagger' (instrument). ببرید here is the verb (an older/poetic form of بریدن 'to cut' / 'to sever') meaning 'cut off' or 'slay'; the lemma senses supplied in the dataset do not reflect this verbal sense, so they do not align. پست is an adjective 'low/despicable' modifying an implied address or the manner of the action.
segments.s401-g23: خود 'he himself' is emphatic subject; و بیست مرد 'and twenty men' coordinates a numeric phrase. از دلیران is a partitive/genitive 'of the brave (men)' and گرد here functions as a nominal/adverbial meaning 'together/around' (i.e. they gathered as a group). The sequence describes who assembled into the small party.
segments.s401-g24: بشد is a past/epic perfective of شدن 'it/he became' or a narrative 'he was/made himself', here introducing the resulting action. تیز 'swift/fast' describes his movement (bشد تیز = 'he moved swiftly'). و دیگر بدیشان سپرد is coordinated: دیگر 'the rest/others', بدیشان 'to them', and سپرد 'he entrusted/left' (past of سپردن). So: 'he sped off and entrusted the others to them.'
segments.s401-g57: This is a past plural clause with two adverbial quantifiers before the verb: پیاپی (repeatedly) + بسی (an intensifier/quantifier) modify the compound object تیغ و خنجر (sword and dagger). زدند is simple past plural 'they struck/they struck at'. The noun phrase تیغ و خنجر is the direct object (no را), coordinated by و.
segments.s401-g58: The pair گهی ... گاه forms a correlative adverbial 'sometimes ... sometimes ...'. Each is followed by a prepositional phrase introduced by بر ('on/at') marking the target of the strike (بر میان = 'on the middle/chest', بر سر = 'on the head'). The final زدند (past plural) applies to both halves of the correlative structure.
segments.s401-g59: به زخم اندر is an instrumental/agentive prepositional phrase ('by/through a wound within'). ارجاسپ را is a definite object marked by را (here marking the person affected), and کرد سست is a causative construction: کرد (caused/made) + adjective سست ('weak'). In older/poetic Persian this order (prepositional phrase — object — کرد adjective) is common.
segments.s401-g60: ندیدند is past plural with the negative prefix ن- (they did not see / they found no). بر تنش is a prepositional phrase 'on his body' (بر + تن + enclitic ش 'his'). جایی درست is a noun phrase 'a proper/intact place' functioning as the object of ندیدند: 'they saw no proper place on his body.'
segments.s401-g61: ز پای اندر آمد is an idiomatic, somewhat poetic expression: ز (from) + پای (foot/feet) + اندر آمد (literally 'came in' but here 'gave way'/'fell down'), so the clause means 'his legs gave way / he fell.' تن پیلوار places the noun 'body' with the word پیلوار (an adverbial/adjectival classical word meaning 'continuously/successively' or describing the manner) following it; together they describe the body collapsing/falling.
segments.s401-g62: جدا کردش is a two-word verbal construction: جدا (apart/severed) + کردش (past 'he did it to him', with enclitic ش = 'him'). از تن introduces the source 'from the body', and سر اسفندیار is 'the head of Esfandiyār' (possessive expressed by placing the possessor after the noun). The whole means 'he severed the head from Esfandiyār's body.'
segments.s401-g63: چو شد کشته is a temporal subordinate clause 'when (he) was killed': چو = 'when', شد is the auxiliary 'was/become' used here with the passive participle کشته ('killed'). ارجاسپ is the subject and آزرده‌جان is a postpositive epithet meaning 'sore‑hearted' or 'distressed in spirit.'
segments.s401-g64: خروشی برآمد is a common collocation 'a cry/clamor arose.' ز کاخ زنان is a prepositional phrase 'from the palace of the women' (ز = from; کاخ زنان = 'women's palace' where the possessor follows the noun). The clause states the source and nature of the outcry.
segments.s401-g81: برفتند (third-person plural past) is the main verb 'they departed/rode off'. زانجا is an adverb meaning 'from there' placed after the verb for poetic order. صد و شست مرد is a numeric noun phrase 'one hundred and sixty men' where صد 'hundred' is joined by و 'and' to شست (an archaic form of 'sixty') and مرد is the head noun; را is omitted because it's not needed here.
segments.s401-g82: گزیده modifies سواران as an adjective meaning 'chosen/selected', forming the compound 'chosen horsemen'. روز نبرد is a genitive-like temporal phrase 'for the day of battle' (روز = day, نبرد = battle), placed after the noun phrase to state the purpose/time.
segments.s401-g83: همان خواهران را is the definite object 'those same sisters' marked by the object marker را. بر اسپان نشاند contains بر + اسپان 'upon the horses' and نشاند (archaic perfect of نشاندن) meaning 'set/caused to sit' — so 'he made those sisters mount/sit upon the horses.' The verb completes the object phrase that precedes it.
segments.s401-g84: ز درگاه ارجاسپ introduces a source/locative 'from the court of Arjasp' (ز = 'from', درگاه = 'royal court', ارجاسپ = proper name). لشکر براند is the clause's verb phrase: لشکر 'the army/host' is the object being affected and براند (from راندن) means 'drove away' or 'caused to move', so the whole clause means 'he drove the host from Arjasp's court.'
segments.s401-g85: وز ایرانیان here functions as an archaic prepositional/partitive phrase 'of/among the Iranians' (وز is an old poetic form; not one of the supplied senses). نامور modifies مرد چند: a descriptive string 'a few renowned men' where چند = 'a few/several'. The whole phrase indicates who (some famed Iranians) are involved.
segments.s401-g86: به دژ ماند is the verbal clause 'remained in/at the fortress' with به functioning as a locative marker 'at/in' and ماند the past stem 'remained'. با ساوه ارجمند attaches as an accompaniment or specification: 'with noble Saveh' (ساوه is a place/name qualified by ارجمند 'esteemed').
segments.s401-g87: چو 'when' introduces a temporal clause; من is the subject. گفت here functions as 'said' (the lexical entry given lists noun senses, but in context it acts as the verb 'said' or nominalized 'my saying'), followed by the subordinate clause از ایدر به بیرون شوم 'I will go out from here' where از ایدر = 'from here', به بیرون = 'to the outside', and شوم is first-person singular subjunctive/potential 'I may become/go' used here to express the speaker's going.
segments.s401-g88: خود و نامدارن is a coordinated subject 'myself and the renowned ones'. به هامون places the action 'to the plain' (هامون = plain), and the final شوم repeats the first-person verb form, giving the sense 'I (and the famed) shall be on the plain' — a parallel clause with subjunctive/optative flavor.
segments.s401-g49: به دست اندرش is a compact locative/possessive phrase: به + دست gives the notion 'in/by the hand' and اندر + ش is the older locative+possessive combination meaning 'in his hand'. The noun phrase خنجر آب‌گون follows, with خنجر (dagger) as the head and the postpositive adjective آب‌گون ('water-colored, bluish/pale') modifying it.
segments.s401-g50: دهن پر ز آواز uses the pattern 'X پر ز Y' (= 'X full of Y'), where ز is the poetic/prepositional 'of/from'. دهن here denotes the mouth or the utterance (i.e. cries), and آواز is the content. The conjunction و links the parallel phrase دل پر ز خون, formed the same way and meaning 'heart full of blood' (i.e. wounded/bleeding heart).
segments.s401-g51: بدو گفت is the familiar classical sequence 'to him (بدو) said (گفت)' — بدو is the dative 'to him' and گفت is the past tense verb 'said'. کز introduces the subordinate clause ('that/from which/who'), and مرد بازارگان is the noun phrase 'the merchant-man' (بازارگان = merchant).
segments.s401-g52: بیابی is the present/subjunctive/imperative form of یابیدن used here as 'may you find / you should find / you will get' (classical hortative/subjunctive sense). کنون is the adverb 'now'. The objects تیغ و دینارگان follow (تیغ = swords/sword); دینارگان refers to dinars/coins or those who have dinars (i.e. money), used here as 'coin(s)/money'. Word order is verb–adverb–objects, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s401-g53: یکی is the indefinite 'someone / a certain one' introducing the action; هدیه is the noun 'gift'. آرمت here appears as a noun (likely an old/poetic name for an item of armor or a named gift) in apposition to هدیه; لهراسپی is the name 'Lohrasp' in the dative/oblique form marking the recipient. The sequence is compact and characteristic of epic diction (gift + recipient).
segments.s401-g54: نهاده is the past/perfect participle/verb 'placed / put' (he put/placed). مهر گشتاسپی 'the seal of Gushtasp' is the noun phrase that follows; مهر (here) = 'seal/stamp' and گشتاسپی is the proper name in genitive (Gushtasp's). برو in this clause functions as an older locative/relational element ('on it / upon him') in the idiomatic phrase 'نهاده برو مهر ...' meaning 'he set/affixed upon it the seal of ...' (the precise etymological shape is archaic/poetic).
segments.s401-g55: برآویخت is a past tense/preterite verb meaning 'rose up / sprang up / launched themselves' — here it governs the compound subject ارجاسپ و اسفندیار. The verb-first order is natural in elevated/poetic Persian and emphasizes the sudden action.
segments.s401-g56: از اندازه is an ablative/measure phrase meaning 'beyond measure' or 'out of measure'. بگذشتشان is the past form with the enclitic -شان ('for them' / 'their') and here conveys 'surpassed/exceeded (for them)'; کارزار 'battle' completes the line — the phrase means essentially 'the battle exceeded measure' (i.e. the fighting went beyond all bounds). Note the slightly inverted order typical of poetry: phrase + verb + enclitic + noun.
segments.s401-g73: شبستان is the direct object 'the bedchamber/inner chamber'. The clause uses the classical Persian order Noun (object) + (enclitic) pronoun + indirect object + verb: او را (the third‑person pronoun with the object marker را, here functioning as an emphatic/possessive reference) + به خادم 'to the servant' + سپرد past tense 'he entrusted/handed over'. The main verb سپرد is simple past and comes at the end.
segments.s401-g74: ازان جایگه is a locative phrase 'from that place/spot' (ازان = 'from that', جایگه = 'place'). رشته‌تایی is a poetic quantifying/ descriptive word meaning 'a strand/a threadlike bit'. نبرد here is an archaic verbal form: the negative prefix ن‑ attached to برد (to take) gives 'نبرد' = 'he did not take' (past tense negative).
segments.s401-g75: در گنج دینار is a locative nominal group 'in the treasury of dinars' (در = 'in', گنج here as 'treasure‑house/vault', دینار = 'dinar(s)'). او is the third‑person pronoun referring to the agent/owner, and مهر کرد is a light verb construction where مهر means 'a seal' (or 'to seal') and کرد is the past 'did/made' → 'he sealed'. The verb phrase is past tense and completes the clause.
segments.s401-g76: به ایوان is locative 'at/on the iwan'. نبودش is a past existential/possessive form with the enclitic -ش (literally 'there was not for him' → 'he had none' or 'there was none'); thus the verb precedes the noun phrase. کسی هم‌نبرد is the noun 'anyone / no one' qualified by هم‌نبرد 'fellow‑warrior / comrade‑in‑arms'. Together: 'At the iwan there was no comrade‑in‑arms (with him).'
segments.s401-g77: بیامد is simple past 'he/one came'. سوی آخر is a directional phrase 'to the far end/side' (سوی = 'toward', آخر = 'end'). و برنشست is 'and he sat (down)' — برنشست is an older/poetic perfect past of نشستن with the prefix بر‑ ('sat upon/took a seat'). The verbs are in past narrative sequence.
segments.s401-g78: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain one / someone'. تیغ هندی is a noun phrase 'an Indian sword' (تیغ = 'sword', هندی = 'Indian' or 'Indian‑style'). گرفته is a past participle/ stative 'having taken / holding'. به دست is a prepositional phrase 'in (his) hand' (the possessor is omitted and understood from context). The whole phrase describes the person: 'a certain man, holding an Indian sword in his hand.'
segments.s401-g79: ازان is 'from that/from among them'. تازی اسپان کش is a compound description: تازی 'Arab(ian)', اسپان 'horses' and کش functioning as an agentive element (‑kesh) producing 'horse‑handler/horse‑puller'. آمد is past 'came'. گزین here functions as a verb 'to choose/select' (so آمد گزین = 'came to choose'). The phrase as a whole: 'An Arab horse‑handler came to choose.'
segments.s401-g80: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past used as 'he ordered/commanded/said'. تا introduces a subordinate purpose/result clause ('that / so that'). برنهادند is a 3rd‑person plural past-subjunctive/imperative-like form from نهادن with the prefix بر‑ meaning 'they should lift/put on / take up' (here 'lift the saddles'), and زین is the object 'saddle'. The structure: 'He ordered that they lift the saddle(s).'

sentences.s401-s53: His entire army stood amazed at him, for the young man displayed such courage.
sentences.s401-s21: His whole palace became such that there was no path in that famed court.
sentences.s401-s22: Because of so many who were exhausted, slain, and battered, the ground was like a troubled sea.
sentences.s401-s23: When Arjasp awoke from sleep, his heart, because of the uproar, became full of anxiety.
sentences.s401-s24: Arjasp seethed up from his place and put on (his) boots and a Roman helmet.
sentences.s401-s33: Such is the conduct of the turning world: at times we find delight from it, at times poison.
sentences.s401-s34: Why do you bind your heart in a sponge-house? Since you know that you will not remain here, do not be upset.
sentences.s401-s35: Esfandiyar dealt with Arjasp; he brought ruin from the iwan up toward the heavens.
sentences.s401-s36: He commanded that candles be sold; they burned on every side of the iwan.
sentences.s401-s49: On the occasion of the victory and the palace's return, give thanks to the Pure God.
sentences.s401-s50: From that vantage he must cast the Turk king's head before the army.
sentences.s401-s51: He came from the fortress with one hundred and sixty men, roaring and seething onto the plain of battle.
sentences.s401-s52: When he reached Peshotan's army, thereupon he cried out praise to the famed man.
sentences.s401-s17: Thus the champion said to the sisters: "Go from here, search, and bring (it)."
sentences.s401-s18: To that place which is my marketplace: there is plenty of gold and silver, and it is my seat.
sentences.s401-s19: Do not accompany me to this battlefield; if I give my head, will I then take the helmet?
sentences.s401-s20: A man came with an Indian sword in his fist and killed someone whom he saw among the brave.
sentences.s401-s1: When the night grew darker, Esfandiyar put on a new battle-garment.
sentences.s401-s2: He unfastened (or opened) the lids of the chests; one man sprang toward those relatives (may it be).
sentences.s401-s3: He brought kebab and a drink; the same war-robe and garments to wear.
sentences.s401-s4: When the bread had been eaten, they gave each one three cups, and from that they became joyful.
sentences.s401-s45: Shut the Turks fast in the fortress — unless, O fortunate one, I have a companion.
sentences.s401-s46: Whenever it occurs to you that I have arrived at that virtuous assembly.
sentences.s401-s47: A tumult must be seen from the vantage of the casket that holds the head and crown of King Gushtasp.
sentences.s401-s48: When the multitude gathers in the fortress against the army, (they are) fleeing and turned back from the battlefield.
sentences.s401-s5: Thus he said: tonight is a night full of affliction; if we win a name from here, it is deserved.
sentences.s401-s6: Strive, and make men fight in battle; seek refuge from calamities with God.
sentences.s401-s7: After that he divided the heroes into three shares; whoever sought renown and battle...
sentences.s401-s8: One share of them was (placed) inside the fortress, that they might make war with anyone.
sentences.s401-s13: A brave, armored warrior came into Arjasp’s court, roaring and acting like a lion.
sentences.s401-s14: When a loud, piercing cry came from the door of the hall, Homay rushed forward before the nobles.
sentences.s401-s15: Aba (the name) brought forth his sister; with blood on his eyelashes he made his face disappear.
sentences.s401-s16: When he drew near Esfandiyar, he saw two veiled figures, like the new spring.
sentences.s401-s9: The other contingent remained until they were at the fortress gate, and they did not cease from fighting and blood‑shedding.
sentences.s401-s10: He told the other contingent about the rebels, saying that they must find a sign from them.
sentences.s401-s11: that those who had been with us, drunk from milk, — cut off their chiefs with the dagger, O base one.
sentences.s401-s12: He himself and twenty men of the brave formed a band; he darted forward and left the others in their care.
sentences.s401-s29: They struck again and again with sword and dagger; sometimes at the chest, sometimes at the head they struck.
sentences.s401-s30: The wound within made Arjasp weak; they found no proper place on his body.
sentences.s401-s31: His legs gave way and his body fell down; the head he severed from Esfandiyār's body.
sentences.s401-s32: When Arjasp was slain, the distressed-hearted, an outcry rose from the women's palace.
sentences.s401-s41: From there departed one hundred and sixty chosen men — the horsemen for the day of battle.
sentences.s401-s42: He had those same sisters seated on the horses and drove the host from Arjasp's court.
sentences.s401-s43: Among the Iranians a few renowned men remained in the fortress with noble Saveh.
sentences.s401-s44: When I said I would go out from here, I and the renowned ones would be on the plain.
sentences.s401-s25: A bluish dagger in his hand, his mouth full of cries and his heart full of blood.
sentences.s401-s26: He said to him that from the merchant you may now obtain swords and coin.
sentences.s401-s27: Someone offered a gift to Lohrasp — the Armat — and set upon it the seal of Gushtasp.
sentences.s401-s28: Arjasp and Esfandiyar sprang up, and the battle passed beyond measure.
sentences.s401-s37: He entrusted the bedchamber to the servant; from that place he did not take even a single strand.
sentences.s401-s38: He sealed the dinars in the treasury; at the iwan there was no comrade‑in‑arms with him.
sentences.s401-s39: Someone came to the far end and sat down — a man with an Indian sword in his hand.
sentences.s401-s40: From them an Arab horse‑handler came to select; he commanded that they lift the saddles.
